description:
You are a cloud-architect who has just finished lifting several legacy web-applications
    into an internal Kubernetes cluster.  
    Before “go-live” the security team has asked you to prepare an
    OFF-LINE, versioned firewall configuration bundle that they will later review and import
    into their privileged automation pipeline.  
    Because you do not have root permission inside this development container,
    you must **NOT** apply the rules; instead you will create the artefacts exactly as
    described below.

    REQUIRED END-STATE
    ------------------
    1. Directory layout
       /home/user/cloud_migration/firewall/
           ├── inbound_policy_v1.rules
           ├── inbound_policy_v2.rules
           ├── apply_firewall.sh   ← must be executable
           └── implementation.log

    2. File: /home/user/cloud_migration/firewall/inbound_policy_v1.rules  
       – plain-text UTF-8.  
       – **Must contain the following seven lines and *nothing else***
         (each line ends with a single “\\n” and there is exactly **one** final newline
         after the word “COMMIT”).

           # Version: v1 firewall rules for migrated stack
           *filter
           :INPUT DROP [0:0]
           :FORWARD DROP [0:0]
           :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
           -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -s 203.0.113.0/24 -j ACCEPT
           -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
           -A INPUT -p icmp -j ACCEPT
           COMMIT

    3. File: /home/user/cloud_migration/firewall/inbound_policy_v2.rules  
       – plain-text UTF-8 with the exact nine lines below (plus one final newline):

           # Version: v2 firewall rules for migrated stack
           *filter
           :INPUT DROP [0:0]
           :FORWARD DROP [0:0]
           :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
           -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -s 203.0.113.0/24 -j ACCEPT
           -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -s 198.51.100.0/24 -j ACCEPT
           -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
           -A INPUT -p icmp -j ACCEPT
           COMMIT

    4. File: /home/user/cloud_migration/firewall/apply_firewall.sh  
       – shell script, **must be marked executable** (mode 755).  
       – When executed with the single positional argument “v1” or “v2” it must
         print **exactly** the three lines shown below (changing only the version):
             Applying firewall policy version: v1
             Using rules file: /home/user/cloud_migration/firewall/inbound_policy_v1.rules
             Simulation complete.
         If run with an invalid argument or with the wrong number of arguments it must
         exit with a non-zero status and emit a helpful usage message that
         begins with the word “Usage:”.

    5. File: /home/user/cloud_migration/firewall/implementation.log  
       – plain-text UTF-8, permissions 644.  
       – Append exactly **three** checksum entries in the order shown; each entry is
         on a single line with a space-delimited timestamp, the filename, two **TAB**
         characters (\\t) and the corresponding *lower-case* SHA-256 digest:

             [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] inbound_policy_v1.rules<TAB><TAB><sha256sum>
             [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] inbound_policy_v2.rules<TAB><TAB><sha256sum>
             [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] apply_firewall.sh<TAB><TAB><sha256sum>

       – Requirements for the agent:
         • Use the current system time for the timestamp in **ISO-8601** “YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS”  
           (24-hour clock, local time).  
         • Compute the SHA-256 checksum *after* each file has been written.  
         • Do not leave a blank line at the end of the log file.

    6. Permissions
       /home/user/cloud_migration                : 755
       /home/user/cloud_migration/firewall       : 755
       *.rules & implementation.log              : 644
       apply_firewall.sh                         : 755

    AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
    ----------------------
    The automated test-suite will:
      a) Confirm the directory tree exists exactly as specified.
      b) Read both *.rules files and perform a byte-for-byte comparison
         against the line-entries shown above.
      c) Ensure “apply_firewall.sh” is executable and, when invoked with
         “v1” and “v2”, emits precisely the required text (no extra spaces
         or blank lines) and exits with status 0.
      d) Verify that “implementation.log” contains three lines in the correct
         format and that the SHA-256 digests listed match the checksums that
         the test suite computes from the three target files.

    YOUR TASK
    ---------
    1. Create the directory structure.
    2. Populate the two *.rules configuration files with the exact contents shown.
    3. Write the executable apply_firewall.sh script that implements the behaviour
       described above.
    4. Calculate the checksums, write the log entries in the mandated format,
       and set all file permissions correctly.

    Do everything from the Linux shell; feel free to use any
    standard command-line tools available in a default Debian/Ubuntu
    environment (e.g., mkdir, echo, cat, awk, sha256sum, chmod, etc.).
truth:
EXPECTED FILE CONTENTS
    ----------------------

    1. /home/user/cloud_migration/firewall/inbound_policy_v1.rules
       (9 lines, each ending with “\n”):

    # Version: v1 firewall rules for migrated stack
    *filter
    :INPUT DROP [0:0]
    :FORWARD DROP [0:0]
    :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
    -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -s 203.0.113.0/24 -j ACCEPT
    -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
    -A INPUT -p icmp -j ACCEPT
    COMMIT
    <NL>

    2. /home/user/cloud_migration/firewall/inbound_policy_v2.rules
       (10 lines, each ending with “\n”):

    # Version: v2 firewall rules for migrated stack
    *filter
    :INPUT DROP [0:0]
    :FORWARD DROP [0:0]
    :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
    -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -s 203.0.113.0/24 -j ACCEPT
    -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -s 198.51.100.0/24 -j ACCEPT
    -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
    -A INPUT -p icmp -j ACCEPT
    COMMIT
    <NL>

    3. /home/user/cloud_migration/firewall/apply_firewall.sh
       (including trailing newline):

    #!/usr/bin/env bash
    # apply_firewall.sh - offline firewall policy switcher
    set -euo pipefail

    if [ "$#" -ne 1 ]; then
      echo "Usage: $0 <v1|v2>"
      exit 1
    fi

    case "$1" in
      v1)
        RULES_FILE="/home/user/cloud_migration/firewall/inbound_policy_v1.rules"
        ;;
      v2)
        RULES_FILE="/home/user/cloud_migration/firewall/inbound_policy_v2.rules"
        ;;
      *)
        echo "Invalid version: $1"
        exit 1
        ;;
    esac

    echo "Applying firewall policy version: $1"
    echo "Using rules file: ${RULES_FILE}"
    echo "Simulation complete."
    <NL>

    4. Permissions that the test will assert (octal):
       /home/user/cloud_migration                          0755
       /home/user/cloud_migration/firewall                 0755
       *.rules & implementation.log                        0644
       apply_firewall.sh                                   0755

    5. implementation.log verification:
       The test harness will:
       • Parse each of the three lines, extract the timestamp (ignored for
         content correctness), the filename and the recorded SHA-256 digest.
       • Re-calculate the digest with `sha256sum` and compare it with the
         recorded value.
       • Ensure the filename portion equals exactly one of:
           inbound_policy_v1.rules
           inbound_policy_v2.rules
           apply_firewall.sh
